paper.title: Beyond Static Leaderboards: Predictive Validity for the Evaluation of LLM Agents

claims.claim_text: Aggregate-score leaderboards collapse orthogonal deployment-relevant dimensions. A Pass1 score of 0.75 can be achieved by many qualitatively different configurations.

evidence.quote_text: A Pass1 score of 0.75 can be achieved by many qualitatively different configurations; one that is reasoning-heavy and cost-expensive, one that is retrieval-rich and latency-bound, one that is tool-hygiene-fragile but artifact-reuse-efficient. Aggregate scores treat these as equivalent; deployment treats them as not.

claims.claim_text: Reasoning-on vs reasoning-off configurations differ by 31 percentage points on clarity-specific scoring while overall rubric mean hides the difference.

evidence.quote_text: Reasoning-on vs. reasoning-off configurations score similarly on overall rubric mean but differ by 31 percentage points on clarity-specific scoring, while data-retrieval and agent-sequence dimensions are unchanged

claims.claim_text: Plan-Execute and Supervisor-Specialist architectures differ by 4.2× on turn-2-to-5 latency due to cross-turn artifact reuse, a dimension invisible in single-turn benchmarks.

evidence.quote_text: Plan-Execute and Supervisor-Specialist architectures score similarly on single-turn Pass1 but differ by 4.2× on turn-2-to-5 latency due to cross-turn artifact reuse; a dimension invisible in single-turn benchmarks

claims.claim_text: Single-pass RAG shows 50–68% accuracy vs agentic multi-hop retrieval ~90% accuracy with 4.5×–10× token inflation. Neither dominates; the right choice depends on deployment constraints.

evidence.quote_text: Single-pass RAG and agentic multi-hop retrieval show 50–68% vs. ~90% accuracy with 4.5×–10× token inflation. Neither dominates; the right choice depends on deployment constraints aggregate scores do not surface.

claims.claim_text: LLM-as-judge measurement is reflexive with model-specific biases. As judge models evolve, ranking shifts; as judge prompts are adjusted, scores move.

evidence.quote_text: Most leaderboards depend on LLM-as-judge scoring (Zheng et al., 2023), which is itself a measurement instrument with model-specific biases. As judge models evolve, ranking shifts; as judge prompts are adjusted, scores move. The leaderboard risks measuring its own judge as much as the systems it evaluates.

claims.claim_text: Condition Insight reports CAR moving from 0.68 to 0.91 under constrained-prompting design — a 20pp improvement attributable to prompting rather than backbone-model choice.

evidence.quote_text: Condition Insight introduces the Condition Agreement Rate (CAR) by comparing LLM-assigned classifications to a parallel rule-based pipeline; reported CAR moves from 0.68 to 0.91 under a constrained-prompting design, a 20pp improvement attributable to prompting rather than to backbone-model choice.

claims.claim_text: ARE/Gaia2 DAG oracle achieves 0.99 precision and 0.95 recall against 450 hand-labeled trajectories.

evidence.quote_text: ARE/Gaia2 checks executed trajectories against human-annotated oracle DAGs with hard, causality, and timing checks that require no LLM evaluation; the reported verifier achieves 0.99 precision and 0.95 recall against 450 hand-labeled trajectories.

claims.claim_text: PHMForge: LLM-as-judge inter-rater reliability reaches only Krippendorff α=0.61, well below the human–human range α∈[0.74,0.82] on the same sample.

evidence.quote_text: In the PHMForge benchmark, LLM-as-judge inter-rater reliability on a 30-scenario stratified sample reaches only Krippendorff α=0.61, well below the human–human range α∈[0.74,0.82] on the same sample.

claims.claim_text: CODS-2025 execution-track public–hidden Spearman ρ=−0.13 (n=13, p=0.71, statistically indistinguishable from zero).

evidence.quote_text: Patel et al. (2026) analyze the CODS-2025 AssetOpsBench challenge (149 teams). Public–hidden Spearman is ρ=0.69 for planning (n=20, robustly positive) and ρ=−0.13 for execution (n=13, p=0.71, statistically indistinguishable from zero).

claims.claim_text: CODS-2025 planning-track public–hidden Spearman ρ=0.69 (n=20), but mean private scores still fall 11.3 points below public, and public scores saturate (8 unique values across 20 teams).

evidence.quote_text: The planning track is where the evidence is robust: rankings do correlate, but mean private scores still fall 11.3 points below public, and public scores saturate (8 unique values across 20 teams).

claims.claim_text: Exgentic observed cross-benchmark rank correlations of 0.32–0.85 across six heterogeneous benchmarks.

evidence.quote_text: Exgentic's analysis observed cross-benchmark rank correlations of 0.32–0.85 across six heterogeneous benchmarks, concluding that 'current architectures do not achieve robust generalization but instead optimize for specific task distributions.'

claims.claim_text: No single prior benchmark reports more than four or five of the twelve tiers. The deployment-extension tiers (T8–T12) are absent from almost all prior benchmarks.

evidence.quote_text: Figure 2: Benchmark × tier coverage. No single prior benchmark reports more than four or five tiers; the deployment-extension tiers (T8–T12) are absent from almost all of them.

claims.claim_text: The twelve tiers are not invented but consolidated from the distinctive contributions of seven source benchmarks plus fourteen parallel implementation studies. Each tier corresponds to a non-redundant measurement dimension.

evidence.quote_text: The twelve tiers in Figure 2 are not invented but consolidated from the distinctive contributions of seven source benchmarks plus fourteen parallel implementation studies. Each tier corresponds to a measurement dimension that at least one prior work surfaces and that we argue is non-redundant with the others.

claims.claim_text: Reasoning-on raises total latency by 21.5% and planning latency by 41.9%. Clarity improves 31pp (61% → 92%), hallucination drops 7pp (12% → 5%), but data retrieval and agent-sequence correctness are unchanged.

evidence.quote_text: Reasoning-on raised total latency by 21.5% (15.08s to 18.32s) and planning latency by 41.9%. The quality gain was non-uniform: clarity improved 31 percentage points (61% to 92%) and hallucination dropped 7pp (12% to 5%), but data retrieval and agent-sequence correctness were unchanged.

claims.claim_text: Knowledge Plugin achieves ~90% accuracy at 114–146s with 4.5×–10× token inflation vs RAG 50–68% accuracy at 8.9–20s. Neither configuration dominates.

evidence.quote_text: With Llama-4-Maverick-17B, RAG reached 50–68% rubric accuracy at 8.9–20s end-to-end, while the Knowledge Plugin reached ~90% at 114–146s with 4.5×–10× token inflation.

claims.claim_text: Replacing MCP tool execution with text-RAG over telemetry-equivalent evidence collapsed remaining-useful-life pass-all-3 from 100% to 20% (5/5 to 1/5) on the lithium-ion class.

evidence.quote_text: Replacing MCP tool execution with text-RAG over telemetry-equivalent evidence collapsed remaining-useful-life pass-all-3 from 100% to 20% (5/5 to 1/5) on the lithium-ion class.

claims.claim_text: Cross-equipment transfer drops pass rate from 84.1% on bearings to 42.7% on motors, a 41-point gap on the same scenario format.

evidence.quote_text: Cross-equipment transfer dropped pass rate from 84.1% on bearings to 42.7% on motors, a 41-point gap on the same scenario format.

claims.claim_text: Frontier LLMs are 'stronger at calling tools than at planning when to call them.'

evidence.quote_text: Frontier LLMs in the authors' words are 'stronger at calling tools than at planning when to call them.'

claims.claim_text: Four independent teams (G7, G9, G12, G27) independently identify MCP-stdio subprocess overhead as a dominant per-call latency floor.

evidence.quote_text: Groups G7, G9, G12, and G27 independently measure MCP-stdio subprocess overhead as a dominant per-call latency floor. G7 reports a 5-6s residual per call. G9 measures discovery as 2-3s per query, eliminated by disk cache. G12 finds direct in-process tools (AT-I) outperform MCP (AT-M) on both pass and judge mean. G27 measures model-loading 233ms → 26ms via pre-loading.

claims.claim_text: Scenario-corpus size (the original 141 scenarios) is the binding constraint on extension work in the field.

evidence.quote_text: Groups G7, G8, G12, and G30 all confront the same problem: the original 141-scenario corpus is small. G7 settles for 15 hand-authored battery scenarios. G12 freezes a 36-scenario paper-grade set. G8 automates scenario generation. G30 scales SME-authored scenarios to 99 across eight asset classes. Scenario-corpus size is the binding constraint on extension work in the field.

claims.claim_text: The base architecture is under-designed, not near-optimal, as evidenced by five convergence patterns across fourteen independent teams.

evidence.quote_text: Fourteen parallel implementation teams plus seven prior benchmark papers converged on the same set of measurement dimensions, each from a distinct architectural starting point. This convergence is the strongest evidence we offer for the synthesis.

claims.claim_text: Three concrete OOD criteria (held-out, cross-subset, adversarial) operationalize predictive validity testing.

evidence.quote_text: Criterion A: Held-Out Scenarios (mild shift). Criterion B: Cross-Subset Transfer (moderate shift). Criterion C: Adversarial Perturbation (strongest shift).

claims.claim_text: The Predictive Validity score combines in-sample mean with OOD reliability: PV(c) = α·Ȳ_c − β·σ_{Y_c,OOD} − γ·IQR(Y_c).

evidence.quote_text: PV(c) = α·Ȳ_c − β·σ_{Y_c,OOD} − γ·IQR(Y_c) where Ȳ_c is in-sample mean, σ_{Y_c,OOD} is cross-OOD-criterion standard deviation of rank position, and IQR(Y_c) is interquartile range of per-scenario scores.

claims.claim_text: Four falsification conditions with explicit thresholds for the position to be considered well-supported: ρ<0.85 across ≥2 OOD criteria; top-3 leave top-5 in ≥10% splits; mean-OOD-variance ρ_Pearson>0.2; PV-top-10 vs mean-top-10 Jaccard <0.85.

evidence.quote_text: Spearman rank correlation between in-sample and OOD rankings ρ<0.85 across at least two of three OOD criteria. Top-3 in-sample configurations leave top-5 on out-of-sample in ≥10% of holdout splits. Mean-vs-OOD-variance correlation ρ_Pearson>0.2. PV-ranking top-10 differs from mean-ranking top-10 with Jaccard overlap <0.85.

claims.claim_text: SmartGridBench 2,420-trajectory experiment shows MCP standardization adds latency without quality gain, while orchestration changes raise pass rate from 43.2% to 55.5%.

evidence.quote_text: In a 2,420-trajectory experiment over the plan-execute base agent, varying transport (direct vs. MCP) and orchestration (Plan-Execute vs. Verified PE vs. Self-Ask) independently shows that MCP standardization adds latency without quality gain, while orchestration changes alone raise pass rate from 43.2% to 55.5%.

claims.claim_text: QLoRA fine-tuning: AT-F1 0.65 vs baseline 0.47, tokens reduced 82.6%; catastrophic forgetting: Gemma retains 79.8% MCQ accuracy, Qwen3 retains 61.3%.

evidence.quote_text: The fine-tuned model reached AT-F1 0.65 versus the baseline's 0.47 and judge score 3.88 versus 2.88, with an 82.6% reduction in input tokens. A catastrophic-forgetting probe exposed: Gemma retained 79.8% of pre-training MCQ accuracy, while Qwen3-4B under the same protocol retained only 61.3%.

claims.claim_text: Confidence-gated routing at θ=0.8: overall correct 13.0% → 30.4%, hallucination 93.5% → 35.6%, agent-sequence correctness 6.5% → 88.9%.

evidence.quote_text: At that threshold, overall-correct rose from 13.0% to 30.4%, hallucination fell from 93.5% to 35.6%, and agent-sequence correctness rose from 6.5% to 88.9%.

claims.claim_text: Visual inspection extension: AWQ W4A16 + domain calibration achieves pass 0.48 → 0.82 with 1.99× latency reduction; FP8 KV-cache causes 0/44 response collapse on Qwen2.5-VL-7B.

evidence.quote_text: AWQ W4A16 with domain-specific calibration: Qwen pass rate 0.48→0.82 with 1.99× latency reduction. An aggressive vLLM serving bundle combining FP8 KV-cache, chunked prefill, and prefix caching collapsed Qwen's output entirely (0/44, response collapse).

claims.claim_text: Multi-turn artifact reuse: Supervisor-Specialist drops tool-time from 47.3% to 26.3%, planning effectiveness 0.559→0.791, schema-failure rate −68.7%, turns 2–5 ran 4.2× faster.

evidence.quote_text: SS dropped tool-time share of wall-clock from 47.3% to 26.3%, planning effectiveness rose from 0.559 to 0.791, schema-failure rate fell 68.7%, and turns 2–5 ran 4.2× faster than turn 1 (34.3s vs. 145.4s mean).
